content.full_text:
Re
Farn PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs ConrrvrrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
October 4,2002
The Honorable George C. Runner, Jr
California State Assembly
Post Office Box942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-000 I
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. l-02-267
Dear Assemblymemb er Runner:
This letter is in response to your request for assistance regarding the provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1 While the Commission is authoizedio provide
written advice to any person whose duties under the Act are in question or to that
person's authorized representative, the Commission does not provide advice to third
parties about a public official's duties under the Act. (Section 83ll4;regulation lg32g,
copy enclosed.) However, we provide you the following general information about the
Act, which we hope will be of assistance to you.
QUESTION
You request advice regarding a local hospital district election. There is currently
an individual seeking election to the local hospital district board of directors who is a
physician, and is employed by a healthcare organizationunder contract with the hospital.
You ask how the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would apply to this individual
should he or she be elected.
DISCUSSION
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from makin g, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. I-02-267
Page No. 2
Section 87100 does not prohibit any individual from serving in public office by
virtue of his or her profession. In order to determine whether the prohibition in section
87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the following eight-step
analysis.
Step One: Is the individual a 6'public official?r,
A 'public official" is defined broadly to include every naturalperson who is a
"member, officer, ernployee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...."
(Section 82048; regulation I 8701.)
Step Two: WilI the official be 6'making,n.(participating in makingr" or
ttinfluencing" a governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to disqualiff a public official
from "making," o'participating in making," or "influencing" a govemmental decision. If
the determination is made that the public official is not engaging in any of these actions,
then the conflict-of-interest rules are not implicated and the analysis ends. Therefore, the
threshold question in any analysis is whether the public official is "making,"
"participating in making," or "influencing" a governmental decision.
The following standards apply for determining whether the public official is
"making," "paxticipating in making," or "influencing,, a govemmental decision.
Malcing a Governmental Decision: TJnder the analysis prescribed by step two, a
public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation lS702.l(a).)
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: Apublic official "participates
in making a goveflrmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recofitmendations to the decision-maker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Section 87100; regulation 15702.2.)
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules as to whether a public
official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the govemmental decision is within or before the
public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the public offrcial's agency, but the public official is not a decision-maker per
se. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In that case, if "the official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influenco, ffiy manber, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency" then he or she is attempting to influence a govemmental decision. This includes,
but is not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer." (Ibid.)


File No. I-02-267
Page No. 3
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation IB7O2.3ib).) Under
this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to act on behalf of or as the representative of,
his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency,, to
influence a decision. (Ibid.)
If an official does not "make," ,,partictptate in making,,, or,.influence,, a
governmental decision, a conflict of interest will not exist.
Step Three: What are the officialrs economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a mernber of his or her immediate farnily," or on any of *re official,s economic
interests. There are six possible economic interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment2 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a));
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director, fficer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation I 8703. 1 (b));
3. A public official has an economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 1 03 (c); regulation I 3703.3);
5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gtfts to him or her if the
grfts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 rnonths prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule (section g7103, regulation tsio:.s;.
2 4, "'irrd' "ct 
inves&nent' or interest means any investrnent or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child ofa public official, by an agent on behalfofa public official, or by a business "rtity o,
trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.,, (Section 87103.i


File No. I-02-267
Page No. 4
Step tr'our: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
The next step is to determine whether the economic interests will be involved
directly or indirectly in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) A person, including a
business entity or source of income, is directly involved in a decision before an official,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal,or similar
request or;
,,"!'J,',ff "ffi:lflffi ,ft :#,1,::'*%.,:.,i:lnfo",u,o,
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
(Regulation 187 0a.1 @).)
Under the Commission's regulations, business entities and sources of income
which are not directly involved under the rules stated above are considered indirectly
involved for purposes of choosing the materiality standard. (Regulation 18704.1(b).i
Steps Five & Six: Wi[ the financial effect of the decision on the officialrs economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps.
First, the official must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(b)(5), regulation 18705, et seq.) After finding the
applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whethei it is reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Reg. 18700(bX6).)
An effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood
that it will occur- Certainty is not required, but the effect must be more than a mere
possibility. (Regulation 18706; Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development
comm. (1987) 196 cal.App.3d 983, 989; witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 cal.App.Ea sr 7, g22;
Harper Advice Letter, No. 4-96-298.)
Regulation 18705 defines standards to be used when determining whether a
financial effect of an economic interest is material. For example, if theiource of income
was a relatively small business entity that does not have stock which is traded on any
stock exchange, the effect of a decision is considered material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:


File No. I-02-267
Page No. 5
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year
in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year inthe amount of
$5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities
of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to discuss the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and mateial financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
This exception generally does not apply in cases where economic interests are Jirectly
involved in a decision. Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally
required to participate in the decision. We have enclosed regulations 18707, 18707.1,
and 18708 for your information.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division
Enclosures
Jwwjg
